title: Under-Trained Models in Production
concept: While compute-optimal models are cheap to train, in production we often train smaller models on far more data (over-training) to reduce inference latency and cost.

exercises.prompt: What does it mean when an AI model is in 'production'?

options.text: Running live in an app to serve real users.
Writing the initial code for the model.

exercises.explain: In AI, 'production' refers to the stage where the model is live and actively answering questions from real users.

cards.title: Fast AI on Your Phone
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how giant AI brains fit onto your phone or respond in milliseconds? It's not because they were cheap to build, but because they were trained to be small yet incredibly smart! Let's find out how we optimize AI for the real world.

cards.title: Over-Training for Production
cards.body: In AI, a 'compute-optimal' model balances training cost and performance. However, once a model goes to 'production' (used by real people), we care more about speed and running costs. To solve this, we 'over-train' smaller models on extra data. This makes them smart enough to do the job while keeping them fast and cheap to run.

cards.title: The Delivery Van Dilemma
cards.body: Imagine you are buying a delivery van. You could buy a cheap, slow van that burns a lot of fuel on every delivery (a model that was cheap to train but expensive/slow to run). Or, you can spend more money upfront to buy an efficient, fast electric van. Even though it costs more at the start, it saves you money on every single delivery you make in the future!

cards.title: Llama-3 in Action
cards.body: Llama-3 8B is a famous example. Instead of stopping training early, it was trained on 15 trillion words—far past the point of being 'compute-optimal'. This extra training made this small, fast model perform like a much larger, slower one, saving millions of dollars in server costs.

cards.title: The Training Limit Myth
cards.body: Many people believe that once a model reaches its 'compute-optimal' limit during training, you must stop. In reality, continuing to train a smaller model on extra data makes it much smarter and highly efficient to run for real users.

cards.title: Training vs. Running Costs
cards.body: Here is how the choice of model design affects your wallet and user experience when launching an AI app.

right.text: Expensive to train, but small. Super fast and very cheap to run for users.

left.text: Cheap to train, but large. Slow to respond and expensive to run for every user.

exercises.prompt: Why are 'compute-optimal' models sometimes bad for real-world production?

options.text: They are too cheap to train.
They are often too large and expensive to run for millions of users.

exercises.explain: A compute-optimal model is designed to get the most performance out of a fixed training budget, but it might still be too big, slow, and expensive to run when millions of customers start using it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Training a smaller model on more data than usual helps reduce its latency (delay) in production.
exercises.explain: Over-training a smaller model on more data makes it perform much better while keeping its size small, which makes it faster to generate responses.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about production AI costs.
exercises.sentence: We over-train small models to save money during the user ________ stage.
exercises.explain: Over-training a smaller model on lots of data takes longer upfront, but it lowers the cost of serving predictions (inference) to users later.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI models is optimized for fast, cheap production use through over-training?

options.text: AI Assistant A: A massive 70B parameter model trained on 2 trillion tokens.
AI Assistant B: A small 7B parameter model trained on 15 trillion tokens.
AI Assistant C: A tiny 1B parameter model trained on 10 billion tokens.

exercises.explain: AI Assistant B is over-trained because it is a smaller model (7 billion parameters) trained on a massive dataset (15 trillion tokens) to make it smart yet fast.

exercises.prompt: Why do companies choose to spend extra money training a smaller model on massive amounts of data before deploying it to millions of users?

options.text: Smaller models are always cheaper and faster to train initially.
Running a smaller model reduces the cost and delay of every single user query.
Smaller models are more creative and accurate than huge models.
It is impossible to run large models in a web browser.

exercises.explain: While training a smaller model on extra data costs more upfront, it pays off in production because the smaller model runs much faster and uses fewer compute resources for every single user query.
